meta.title: Mercedes C‑Class S206 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes C‑Class S206 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fifth‑generation C‑Class estate — from the M254 1.5‑litre mild‑hybrid petrol to the 680 PS M139L plug‑in hybrid and the OM654 diesel range. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the all‑four‑cylinder line‑up that defines this generation. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C‑Class S206 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S206 estate specifically — including the M254 mild‑hybrid petrol range, the OM654 diesel family, and the AMG‑tuned M139/M139L units. All models are equipped with four‑cylinder engines featuring an integrated starter generator (15 kW electric motor) and a 48‑volt electrical system.

engines.power: 150 kW (204 PS) engine + 95 kW (129 PS) electric
150 kW (204 PS) engine + 95 kW (129 PS) electric
350 kW (476 PS) engine + 150 kW (204 PS) electric

overview.intro: The S206 is the estate body of Mercedes‑Benz's fifth‑generation C‑Class, launched in March 2021 as the successor to the S205. It introduced the MRA2 platform, a longer wheelbase, and — for the first time — an all‑four‑cylinder engine line‑up with every model featuring an integrated starter generator (15 kW electric motor) and a 48‑volt electrical system. A 2023 facelift brought minor styling updates and the arrival of the C 350 e plug‑in hybrid, but the engine range remained substantially unchanged.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: MRA2 (Mercedes‑Benz Rear‑wheel‑drive Architecture, second generation) • Predecessor: S205 (fourth‑generation C‑Class estate) • Successor: Current generation (production ongoing as of 2026) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W206 (saloon), V206 (long‑wheelbase saloon)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S206 estate generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM654 diesel and M254 petrol variants as this generation enters its fifth year of production.

items.quote: “The 200 PS version of Mercedes’ modular diesel family delivers effortless estate‑car torque with proven longevity — taxi‑fleet examples regularly exceed 350,000 km. The 48V mild‑hybrid system smooths out stop‑start and low‑speed running.” Who it’s for: Buyers who need diesel economy and six‑figure mileage potential.

“The 1.5‑litre mild‑hybrid petrol is the sweet spot of the S206 range — 204 PS is plenty for the estate’s weight, the 48V system provides instant torque off the line, and the reliability record is better than the M264 it replaced.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a petrol S206 without the AMG running costs.

“The most powerful four‑cylinder production car engine ever — but also one of the most complex. The plug‑in hybrid system adds battery, electric motor, and cooling system failure points on top of the high‑stress turbo engine. A full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: Performance buyers with deep pockets and warranty cover.

“Electric motor, battery pack, turbocharger, and engine all in one package — any major failure triggers a cascade of costs. The 6.1 kWh battery alone is a four‑figure replacement.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the complexity and have full warranty cover.

“The 163 PS entry‑level diesel shares the same robust OM654 architecture as the more powerful versions — but costs less to buy and less to replace. DPF and EGR maintenance is the only real demand.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy on a tighter budget.

“The plug‑in hybrid C 300 e offers 204 PS from the petrol engine plus 129 PS from the electric motor — 245 kW combined. It’s often dismissed as a compromise, but for company‑car drivers it’s the most tax‑efficient S206 you can buy.” Who it’s for: Company‑car buyers who prioritise BIK tax over outright performance.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — more power, similar risks
Solid Choice — more power, same architecture

economicsRule.title: The S206 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,200 OM654 recon against a £25,000 S206 sits at 17% — safe. A £8,000 M139 recon against a £30,000 S206 C 43 is 27% — still safe, but the running costs tell a different story. Against a £40,000 C 63 S, a £14,000 M139L recon is 35% — viable if you have the warranty, but a financial event regardless. Value the car and the warranty first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S206 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM654 (C 200 d) — cheap DPF/EGR maintenance, proven architecture.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S206 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M139L (AMG C 63 S E Performance) — battery, electric motor, turbo, and engine all in one complex package.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S206 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM654 (C 220 d) — proven diesel refinement without the hybrid complexity or AMG costs.

problems.title: M254 Cooling System & Electrical Gremlins
problems.rootCause: The M254’s 48V mild‑hybrid system adds complexity — cooling system leaks from plastic fittings, water pump failures, and electrical gremlins (sensor errors, start‑stop malfunctions) are the most common complaints. The integrated starter generator (ISG) can also fail, leaving the car without electrical assistance.
problems.recommendation: If your S206 petrol develops a coolant warning light or intermittent electrical faults, get the cooling system and 48V components inspected immediately — a £400–£1,200 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000 replacement that follows an overheating event.

cta.label: Compare M254 engine prices for your S206 C 200 →

problems.title: OM654 DPF / EGR Clogging
problems.rootCause: The OM654 diesel is sensitive to short‑trip urban driving — the DPF struggles to regenerate without regular motorway runs, and the EGR valve clogs with carbon deposits. The 48V mild‑hybrid system doesn’t solve this fundamental diesel issue.
problems.recommendation: If your S206 diesel is used mainly for urban driving, schedule a DPF inspection every 20,000 miles — a £400–£1,200 preventative clean is far cheaper than the £2,000–£4,200 replacement that follows a blocked DPF.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your S206 C 220 d →

problems.title: M139 / M139L Turbo & Hybrid System Complexity
problems.affectedModels: S206 AMG C 43, AMG C 63 S E Performance (2022–present).
problems.rootCause: The M139’s electrically‑assisted turbocharger (e‑turbo) adds complexity — the electric motor that spools the turbo can fail, and the cooling system struggles to manage the heat. On the C 63 S, the plug‑in hybrid system adds a 6.1 kWh battery and a 150 kW electric motor on the rear axle — multiple additional failure points.
problems.recommendation: If you own an S206 AMG C 43 or C 63 S, treat turbo and cooling system inspections as annual maintenance — £800–£2,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500–£14,000 replacement that follows a neglected turbo or hybrid failure.

cta.label: Compare M139 engine prices for your S206 AMG C 43 →

problems.title: M254 PHEV Battery Degradation (C 300 e / C 350 e)
problems.rootCause: The 25.4 kWh lithium‑ion battery pack degrades over time — range decreases, charging times increase, and eventually the battery management system may flag errors. Battery replacement is a significant cost.
problems.recommendation: If you own an S206 PHEV, get the battery health checked annually — a £500–£1,500 preventative check is far cheaper than the £3,000–£6,000 replacement that follows a failed battery pack.

cta.label: Compare C 300 e battery prices for your S206 →

replacementCosts.h2: S206 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — S206 estate shares identical engine‑bay access with its W206 saloon sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S206 estate only. W206 saloon and V206 long‑wheelbase saloon share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The All‑Terrain variant (raised estate) shares the same engine range.
